title: Mass Assignment in JSON APIs: Object Boundary Control and Safe Update Contracts
excerpt: How to prevent privilege and workflow abuse caused by implicit field binding in REST and GraphQL mutation handlers.
content:
# Mass Assignment in JSON APIs: Object Boundary Control and Safe Update Contracts

## Audience and Level
- Target audience: backend engineers, API security reviewers, platform owners
- Difficulty: specialist
- Estimated read time: 15 minutes

## Why This Matters
Mass assignment flaws appear when frameworks automatically bind user-controlled fields into domain objects without explicit allowlists. The bug often starts as a convenience optimization and later becomes a privilege escalation vector when new sensitive properties are introduced. Attackers do not need code execution; they only need write access to an endpoint that accepts structured input and silently maps attributes.

## Threat Model Snapshot
- Assets at risk: role flags, billing limits, verification states, internal ownership fields
- Entry points: PATCH and PUT endpoints, profile updates, admin-like mutation endpoints
- Trust boundaries: external JSON payloads to ORM model updates
- Typical attacker goals: escalate privileges, bypass workflow gates, modify protected business state

## Attack Mechanics
Attackers enumerate writable fields by observing error behavior, diffing responses, and replaying requests with additional keys. They probe nested objects and polymorphic payloads where serializer defaults are permissive. In many incidents, the first successful abuse is not role=admin; it is a lower-noise field such as account_tier, email_verified, owner_id, or credit_limit that later enables deeper compromise.

## Detection and Verification
- Manual checks: inspect serializer or DTO definitions for explicit writable allowlists
- Dynamic checks: send unauthorized properties and compare persisted object state
- Telemetry indicators: repeated mutation attempts with non-documented fields
- False positive control: confirm protected field mutation without elevated authorization

## Secure Implementation
Use explicit input contracts per endpoint and avoid model-wide update operations driven directly by request bodies. Separate public mutation DTOs from internal domain entities. Enforce field-level authorization checks for every sensitive attribute and reject unknown keys with strict schema validation. Add invariant checks in business logic so privileged fields cannot be set through user-driven channels, even if validation is accidentally relaxed in the future.

## Hardening Checklist
- [x] Endpoint-specific writable field allowlists
- [x] Unknown-field rejection and strict schema mode
- [x] Field-level authorization for sensitive attributes
- [x] Business-layer invariants that block unsafe state transitions
- [x] Regression tests for protected field mutation attempts

## Mapping and References
- OWASP: API security and broken access control guidance
- CWE: CWE-915, CWE-639
- WSTG: authorization and API testing workflows
- ASVS: access control and data validation requirements

## References
1. https://owasp.org/API-Security/editions/2023/en/0xa6-mass-assignment/
2. https://owasp.org/Top10/2025/
3. https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/v42/
4. https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/915.html
author: Security Team

title: Open Redirect Chains: OAuth Abuse, Cookie Scope Confusion, and Reliable Validation
excerpt: Specialist treatment of open redirect risk when combined with login flows, token return paths, and browser trust cues.
content:
# Open Redirect Chains: OAuth Abuse, Cookie Scope Confusion, and Reliable Validation

## Audience and Level
- Target audience: identity teams, frontend security engineers, application defenders
- Difficulty: specialist
- Estimated read time: 14 minutes

## Why This Matters
Open redirects are often dismissed as low severity in isolation. In real systems they become high impact when chained with OAuth callbacks, password reset links, phishing lures, and trust transfer between subdomains. Attackers weaponize legitimate domains to increase click-through probability and reduce user suspicion.

## Threat Model Snapshot
- Assets at risk: authorization codes, reset tokens, user trust, session continuity
- Entry points: next or returnUrl parameters, login handoff routes, email action links
- Trust boundaries: user-supplied redirect targets to server redirect responses
- Typical attacker goals: steal tokens, capture credentials, bypass anti-phishing controls

## Attack Mechanics
Attackers identify endpoints that accept arbitrary destinations or weakly validated relative paths. They then craft chained URLs that begin on trusted domains and end on attacker infrastructure. In OAuth contexts, open redirects can help bypass strict redirect expectations if intermediate handlers preserve sensitive query parameters.

## Detection and Verification
- Manual checks: inventory all redirect-capable routes and validation rules
- Dynamic checks: test absolute, scheme-relative, encoded, and mixed-case payloads
- Telemetry indicators: repeated redirects to rarely seen external domains
- False positive control: demonstrate sensitive parameter leakage through redirect chain

## Secure Implementation
Adopt a positive allowlist for redirect destinations and treat any external redirect as a privileged action. Normalize URLs before validation to prevent encoding bypasses, and strip sensitive parameters before performing any redirect operation. Use signed return-state values when preserving post-auth navigation intent.

## Hardening Checklist
- [x] Allowlist-only external redirect policy
- [x] URL normalization before validation
- [x] Sensitive parameter stripping and one-time state values
- [x] OAuth callback routing with strict destination constraints
- [x] Redirect abuse detection rules in monitoring pipeline

## Mapping and References
- OWASP: unvalidated redirect guidance and authentication controls
- CWE: CWE-601, CWE-346
- WSTG: authentication and redirect testing tracks
- ASVS: trusted navigation and input validation controls

## References
1. https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Unvalidated_Redirects_and_Forwards_Cheat_Sheet.html
2. https://portswigger.net/kb/issues/00500100_open-redirection-reflected
3. https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/v42/
4. https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/601.html
author: Security Team

title: Session Fixation in Federated Login: Pre-Auth Session Rotation and Token Binding
excerpt: Defensive patterns for preventing attacker-controlled session continuity across SSO and account-linking flows.
content:
# Session Fixation in Federated Login: Pre-Auth Session Rotation and Token Binding

## Audience and Level
- Target audience: authentication service engineers and IAM teams
- Difficulty: specialist
- Estimated read time: 15 minutes

## Why This Matters
Session fixation survives in modern stacks when pre-auth identifiers are reused after authentication or account linking events. Federated login adds complexity because the application, identity provider, and browser each contribute state. If rotation and binding are incomplete, attackers can pre-seed sessions and later hijack authenticated context.

## Threat Model Snapshot
- Assets at risk: authenticated sessions, account ownership, privileged action continuity
- Entry points: login initiation endpoints, callback handlers, remember-me tokens
- Trust boundaries: anonymous browser state to authenticated server session
- Typical attacker goals: hijack post-login session without stealing credentials directly

## Attack Mechanics
Attackers induce victims to use attacker-known session identifiers through crafted links or forced browsing steps. If the server fails to rotate session IDs after successful authentication, the attacker can resume the same identifier and inherit authenticated state. In federated flows this risk increases when multiple cookies or token caches are inconsistently rotated.

## Detection and Verification
- Manual checks: verify session ID lifecycle before and after every authentication milestone
- Dynamic checks: fixation simulation with controlled session seeds and cross-browser replay
- Telemetry indicators: concurrent authenticated usage of session IDs across distinct fingerprints
- False positive control: prove attacker access with fixed identifier after victim login

## Secure Implementation
Rotate session identifiers at authentication success, MFA completion, privilege elevation, and sensitive account linking. Bind session state to additional context signals and invalidate old identifiers immediately. Ensure consistent rotation across application sessions, refresh tokens, and gateway cookies.

## Hardening Checklist
- [x] Session ID rotation at all auth transition points
- [x] Invalidation of pre-auth and superseded identifiers
- [x] Binding checks for user agent and risk context
- [x] Federated callback tests covering fixation scenarios
- [x] High-fidelity alerts for concurrent session anomalies

## Mapping and References
- OWASP: session management and authentication guidance
- CWE: CWE-384, CWE-613
- WSTG: session management testing methodology
- ASVS: session lifecycle and re-authentication controls

## References
1. https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/v42/
2. https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Session_Management_Cheat_Sheet.html
3. https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/384.html
4. https://owasp.org/www-project-application-security-verification-standard/
author: Security Team

title: GraphQL BOLA and Resolver Authorization Drift: Policy Placement That Scales
excerpt: A specialist blueprint for eliminating object-level authorization drift across growing GraphQL resolver sets.
content:
# GraphQL BOLA and Resolver Authorization Drift: Policy Placement That Scales

## Audience and Level
- Target audience: GraphQL platform teams and API security engineers
- Difficulty: specialist
- Estimated read time: 15 minutes

## Why This Matters
GraphQL deployments often begin with few resolvers and ad-hoc checks. As schemas grow, authorization becomes inconsistent across nested paths and new mutations. Broken object-level authorization appears when policy logic is repeated manually, causing drift and blind spots that attackers can enumerate with introspection and query shaping.

## Threat Model Snapshot
- Assets at risk: tenant data boundaries, private objects, administrative mutation paths
- Entry points: resolver arguments, node IDs, nested relationship traversal
- Trust boundaries: authenticated identity context to object retrieval logic
- Typical attacker goals: horizontal privilege escalation and cross-tenant data access

## Attack Mechanics
Attackers discover object identifiers through predictable patterns, leaked references, or prior API responses. They then query alternate resolver paths that reach the same objects but bypass checks present in primary endpoints. Alias and batching features can amplify reconnaissance and exploitation speed.

## Detection and Verification
- Manual checks: map object access rules to every resolver path returning that object
- Dynamic checks: replay unauthorized object IDs across queries and mutations
- Telemetry indicators: repeated not-authorized errors around sequential IDs
- False positive control: verify unauthorized access to object content or state change

## Secure Implementation
Centralize authorization decisions in reusable policy services and enforce checks at object fetch boundaries, not just route handlers. Apply deny-by-default behavior for missing policy outcomes. Add schema governance checks that block resolver merges unless required authorization hooks are present.

## Hardening Checklist
- [x] Object-level authorization service reused across resolvers
- [x] Deny-by-default policy behavior
- [x] Resolver coverage tests for cross-tenant object access
- [x] Query cost and depth controls to slow enumeration
- [x] Security review gates on schema evolution

## Mapping and References
- OWASP: API authorization and access control recommendations
- CWE: CWE-639, CWE-285
- WSTG: authorization testing workflows
- ASVS: access control verification requirements

## References
1. https://owasp.org/API-Security/editions/2023/en/0xa1-broken-object-level-authorization/
2. https://portswigger.net/web-security/graphql
3. https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/v42/
4. https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/639.html
author: Security Team

title: Presigned URL Abuse in Object Storage Workflows: Time, Scope, and Replay Control
excerpt: Advanced guidance for securing presigned upload and download flows used by web and mobile applications.
content:
# Presigned URL Abuse in Object Storage Workflows: Time, Scope, and Replay Control

## Audience and Level
- Target audience: cloud platform engineers and backend API teams
- Difficulty: specialist
- Estimated read time: 14 minutes

## Why This Matters
Presigned URLs are powerful for offloading transfer load, but they shift authorization into signed request artifacts. If validity windows are too long, scopes too broad, or object naming predictable, attackers can replay links, overwrite assets, or exfiltrate private content without direct platform credentials.

## Threat Model Snapshot
- Assets at risk: private files, user uploads, compliance-sensitive records
- Entry points: upload initialization endpoints, share links, object retrieval routes
- Trust boundaries: application authorization to storage-layer signed operations
- Typical attacker goals: unauthorized read or write, data replacement, long-lived access

## Attack Mechanics
Attackers obtain presigned links from logs, browser history, referrer leakage, or weakly protected APIs. They replay links from different clients and attempt object key manipulation when signatures permit broader prefixes. In write flows they race legitimate uploads or replace expected media with malicious payloads.

## Detection and Verification
- Manual checks: verify strict key scoping and short expiration defaults
- Dynamic checks: replay and cross-context use of issued links
- Telemetry indicators: link reuse from new geographies or user contexts
- False positive control: prove unauthorized object action with captured URL

## Secure Implementation
Issue presigned URLs with minimal lifetime and tightly constrained operation scope. Bind link issuance to explicit object keys and expected content constraints. Track single-use semantics where feasible and validate uploaded object metadata before making content reachable by end users.

## Hardening Checklist
- [x] Minimal expiration and operation-specific signatures
- [x] Strict object key scoping without wildcard prefixes
- [x] Replay detection and optional one-time usage controls
- [x] Post-upload validation pipeline before publish
- [x] Monitoring on abnormal presigned URL access patterns

## Mapping and References
- OWASP: broken access control and secure file handling guidance
- CWE: CWE-200, CWE-639
- WSTG: authorization and file handling test practices
- ASVS: access control and data protection requirements

## References
1. https://owasp.org/Top10/2025/
2. https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/v42/
3. https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/200.html
4. https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/639.html
author: Security Team

title: Device Authorization Flow Abuse: Phishing-Resistant UX and Token Issuance Safeguards
excerpt: Threat-informed controls for OAuth device flow deployments in TV, CLI, and constrained-device ecosystems.
content:
# Device Authorization Flow Abuse: Phishing-Resistant UX and Token Issuance Safeguards

## Audience and Level
- Target audience: identity providers, consumer platform teams, IAM architects
- Difficulty: specialist
- Estimated read time: 14 minutes

## Why This Matters
OAuth device authorization flow is essential for constrained clients, yet it introduces human-mediated verification steps that are vulnerable to social engineering. Attackers can trick users into entering user codes for attacker-initiated sessions and gain delegated access without touching victim credentials.

## Threat Model Snapshot
- Assets at risk: delegated API scopes, account-linked device sessions, profile data
- Entry points: user code entry pages, polling token endpoints, device activation prompts
- Trust boundaries: user interaction channel to authorization server issuance logic
- Typical attacker goals: obtain authorized tokens through consent phishing

## Attack Mechanics
Attackers initiate device flow on their own clients and present victims with real user codes through deceptive prompts. If user confirmation UX lacks contextual binding, victims may approve unintended devices. Weak rate controls on polling endpoints can amplify abuse and create noisy denial conditions.

## Detection and Verification
- Manual checks: review code entry UX for explicit device and risk context
- Dynamic checks: simulate mismatched device metadata and consent prompts
- Telemetry indicators: unusual authorization completion from unrelated IP contexts
- False positive control: demonstrate token issuance to attacker-controlled client after victim approval

## Secure Implementation
Display strong contextual details during verification, including device type, location hints, and client reputation signals. Apply short code lifetimes, strict polling intervals, and anomaly controls on repeated code failures. Require step-up verification for high-risk scopes and suspicious authorization context changes.

## Hardening Checklist
- [x] Context-rich verification page for user code approval
- [x] Short user code lifetime and anti-bruteforce throttling
- [x] Polling endpoint rate limits and abuse detection
- [x] Step-up controls for privileged scopes
- [x] Security analytics for consent-phishing patterns

## Mapping and References
- OWASP: authentication and secure flow design guidance
- CWE: CWE-287, CWE-307
- WSTG: authentication testing methodology
- ASVS: secure authentication and transaction integrity controls

## References
1. https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc8628
2. https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Authentication_Cheat_Sheet.html
3. https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/v42/
4. https://owasp.org/www-project-application-security-verification-standard/
author: Security Team

title: HTTP/2 Rapid Reset and Layer-7 Resilience: Application-Aware Mitigation Patterns
excerpt: Operational patterns for reducing application impact from modern HTTP/2 abuse and request-flood asymmetry.
content:
# HTTP/2 Rapid Reset and Layer-7 Resilience: Application-Aware Mitigation Patterns

## Audience and Level
- Target audience: platform SRE teams, edge security engineers, backend owners
- Difficulty: specialist
- Estimated read time: 14 minutes

## Why This Matters
Layer-7 denial techniques evolve faster than many application architectures. HTTP/2 rapid reset style abuse can generate disproportionate backend work relative to attacker cost, especially when expensive middleware, auth checks, or dynamic rendering paths are triggered before connection-level controls engage.

## Threat Model Snapshot
- Assets at risk: application availability, latency SLOs, incident response capacity
- Entry points: edge gateways, reverse proxies, expensive dynamic endpoints
- Trust boundaries: network-facing protocols to application compute pools
- Typical attacker goals: service degradation, alert fatigue, and emergency rollback pressure

## Attack Mechanics
Attackers open high volumes of streams and rapidly cancel or reset them, forcing repeated allocation and processing. They target endpoints with heavy server-side work and can blend traffic to bypass simplistic rate rules. Secondary effects include cache churn, thread exhaustion, and retry amplification from downstream clients.

## Detection and Verification
- Manual checks: identify endpoints with high per-request CPU or DB cost
- Dynamic checks: controlled stress tests emulating stream churn and cancellation
- Telemetry indicators: elevated stream resets, queue growth, and latency spikes
- False positive control: reproduce asymmetric load behavior under synthetic attack profile

## Secure Implementation
Implement layered protections: edge-level stream limits, adaptive rate controls, request cost budgeting, and graceful degradation for non-critical features. Prioritize cheap rejection paths before expensive business logic and enforce backpressure between gateway and application services.

## Hardening Checklist
- [x] HTTP/2 stream and reset thresholds tuned at edge
- [x] Endpoint cost classification and protective quotas
- [x] Early request rejection before expensive middleware paths
- [x] Circuit breakers and fallback behavior for critical dependencies
- [x] Routine resilience drills for layer-7 abuse scenarios

## Mapping and References
- OWASP: availability and resilient architecture principles
- CWE: CWE-400, CWE-770
- WSTG: operational testing and abuse simulation
- ASVS: secure architecture and availability controls

## References
1. https://portswigger.net/daily-swig/http-2-rapid-reset-ddos-attack-explained
2. https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/v42/
3. https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/400.html
4. https://owasp.org/www-project-application-security-verification-standard/
author: Security Team

title: Mutual TLS for Internal APIs: Certificate Identity, Rotation, and Failure Modes
excerpt: Practical guidance for mTLS deployments that preserve zero-trust intent without causing brittle production outages.
content:
# Mutual TLS for Internal APIs: Certificate Identity, Rotation, and Failure Modes

## Audience and Level
- Target audience: platform security architects and service owners
- Difficulty: specialist
- Estimated read time: 15 minutes

## Why This Matters
mTLS strengthens service-to-service trust by requiring both sides to present verifiable certificates. Yet many deployments fail operationally when identity mapping, revocation handling, and certificate rotation are not engineered as first-class lifecycle concerns. Security can degrade silently if fallback paths permit unauthenticated traffic during outages.

## Threat Model Snapshot
- Assets at risk: internal APIs, control-plane actions, sensitive east-west traffic
- Entry points: service mesh gateways, direct service calls, legacy side channels
- Trust boundaries: network identity to application authorization policy
- Typical attacker goals: lateral movement, service impersonation, privilege abuse

## Attack Mechanics
Attackers exploit weak certificate validation, permissive trust stores, or fallback listeners that accept plaintext when TLS fails. They target stale certificates, compromised intermediate authorities, and identity mismatches between certificate subject and authorization policy labels.

## Detection and Verification
- Manual checks: verify certificate chain policy and identity-to-policy mapping
- Dynamic checks: test expired, revoked, and mismatched cert scenarios in staging
- Telemetry indicators: handshake failures, fallback route usage, policy bypass anomalies
- False positive control: demonstrate unauthorized service call under misconfigured trust

## Secure Implementation
Pin service identity to strict certificate attributes and enforce fail-closed behavior across gateways and services. Automate certificate issuance and rotation with short lifetimes and robust rollout controls. Maintain emergency response runbooks for CA compromise and trust bundle rollback events.

## Hardening Checklist
- [x] Strict certificate identity mapping to authorization policy
- [x] Automated short-lifetime cert rotation pipeline
- [x] Fail-closed behavior with no plaintext fallback
- [x] Revocation and trust-bundle incident playbooks
- [x] Continuous validation tests for TLS policy drift

## Mapping and References
- OWASP: transport security and secure architecture practices
- CWE: CWE-295, CWE-326
- WSTG: TLS and configuration verification workflows
- ASVS: communications and cryptographic control requirements

## References
1. https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Transport_Layer_Security_Cheat_Sheet.html
2. https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/v42/
3. https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/295.html
4. https://owasp.org/www-project-application-security-verification-standard/
author: Security Team

title: CSP Strict-Dynamic Rollouts: Migration Strategy for Legacy Frontend Dependency Graphs
excerpt: How to move large frontend estates toward nonce-based strict CSP without blocking business-critical releases.
content:
# CSP Strict-Dynamic Rollouts: Migration Strategy for Legacy Frontend Dependency Graphs

## Audience and Level
- Target audience: frontend platform teams, AppSec engineers, release managers
- Difficulty: practitioner
- Estimated read time: 13 minutes

## Why This Matters
Teams know strict CSP is effective against script injection, but migration stalls when legacy bundles, inline scripts, and third-party tags are deeply entangled. A failed rollout can break customer journeys, leading to broad policy relaxation that eliminates security gains.

## Threat Model Snapshot
- Assets at risk: browser execution trust, user sessions, sensitive UI workflows
- Entry points: reflected or stored script injection, unsafe third-party scripts
- Trust boundaries: server-generated markup to browser execution policy
- Typical attacker goals: execute arbitrary scripts and exfiltrate user data

## Attack Mechanics
Attackers exploit any injection point where script execution remains possible through unsafe-inline allowances or overbroad host source lists. During partial migrations, hybrid policies often leave compatibility holes that preserve exploitability while giving teams false confidence.

## Detection and Verification
- Manual checks: inventory inline scripts and dynamic loader behavior by route
- Dynamic checks: CSP report analysis plus targeted XSS simulation payloads
- Telemetry indicators: high CSP violation rates on critical transaction pages
- False positive control: confirm payload execution blocked under enforced strict policy

## Secure Implementation
Adopt nonce-based script control with strict-dynamic and phased enforcement. Start with report-only baselines, then move route by route to enforcement with automated canary checks. Reduce third-party script surface and require governance for new external script dependencies.

## Hardening Checklist
- [x] Route-level CSP inventory and migration sequencing
- [x] Nonce issuance integrated into rendering pipeline
- [x] strict-dynamic and removal of unsafe-inline
- [x] CSP report triage integrated with release gates
- [x] Third-party script onboarding policy with security review

## Mapping and References
- OWASP: XSS prevention and secure browser policy guidance
- CWE: CWE-79, CWE-693
- WSTG: client-side and configuration testing guidance
- ASVS: browser security control requirements

## References
1. https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Content_Security_Policy_Cheat_Sheet.html
2. https://portswigger.net/web-security/cross-site-scripting/content-security-policy
3. https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/v42/
4. https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/79.html
author: Security Team

title: JWT Key Management at Scale: Rotation, Audience Segmentation, and Replay Defense
excerpt: A systems-level approach to JWT hardening that addresses key lifecycle, claim validation, and abuse detection.
content:
# JWT Key Management at Scale: Rotation, Audience Segmentation, and Replay Defense

## Audience and Level
- Target audience: identity platform teams and API security architects
- Difficulty: specialist
- Estimated read time: 15 minutes

## Why This Matters
JWT deployments fail most often in lifecycle and validation details, not in token syntax. Weak key rotation practices, broad audience trust, and missing replay controls allow tokens to persist beyond intended boundaries and create cross-service trust confusion.

## Threat Model Snapshot
- Assets at risk: API authorization integrity, account sessions, privileged service access
- Entry points: bearer token validation middleware, key distribution endpoints, refresh flows
- Trust boundaries: token issuer assumptions to resource server authorization decisions
- Typical attacker goals: reuse stolen tokens, pivot between services, bypass expiration intent

## Attack Mechanics
Attackers exploit lenient claim checks, stale JWKS caches, and overlapping audience definitions. They replay leaked tokens across endpoints that share signing trust but not authorization context. During key rotation windows, misconfigured services may accept deprecated keys longer than expected.

## Detection and Verification
- Manual checks: review validation of iss, aud, exp, nbf, and token type semantics
- Dynamic checks: replay and cross-audience token misuse testing
- Telemetry indicators: token reuse anomalies and issuer-audience mismatch failures
- False positive control: prove unauthorized API access with context-inappropriate token

## Secure Implementation
Use short-lived access tokens, strong refresh token protections, and explicit audience segmentation by service boundary. Automate key rotation with predictable overlap windows and immediate revocation paths for compromise events. Enforce token-type separation so ID tokens are never accepted where access tokens are required.

## Hardening Checklist
- [x] Strict issuer and audience validation in every API
- [x] Automated signing key rotation with monitored cache behavior
- [x] Short token lifetimes and replay-aware revocation controls
- [x] Token type separation across frontend and API boundaries
- [x] Abuse analytics for anomalous token reuse patterns

## Mapping and References
- OWASP: JWT and authentication security guidance
- CWE: CWE-347, CWE-613
- WSTG: token and session testing methodology
- ASVS: authentication and token validation controls

## References
1. https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/JSON_Web_Token_for_Java_Cheat_Sheet.html
2. https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc7519
3. https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/v42/
4. https://owasp.org/www-project-application-security-verification-standard/
author: Security Team

title: JWKS Cache Poisoning and Key Confusion in Multi-Tenant APIs
excerpt: Specialist guidance for preventing key-distribution trust failures in JWT validation pipelines.
content:
# JWKS Cache Poisoning and Key Confusion in Multi-Tenant APIs

## Audience and Level
- Target audience: IAM platform engineers, API gateway teams, security architects
- Difficulty: specialist
- Estimated read time: 15 minutes

## Why This Matters
Many organizations validate JWT signatures correctly but fail at key-distribution trust boundaries. If JWKS retrieval, caching, or issuer-to-key mapping is weak, attackers can introduce trusted-looking keys and bypass authorization in downstream APIs.

## Threat Model Snapshot
- Assets at risk: tenant isolation, privileged API actions, internal service trust
- Entry points: JWKS fetchers, key cache layers, issuer resolution logic
- Trust boundaries: token metadata to key discovery and verification pipelines
- Typical attacker goals: sign attacker-crafted tokens accepted as legitimate

## Attack Mechanics
Attackers target weak kid selection logic, permissive jku or x5u handling, and shared caches that do not isolate issuers. They exploit stale cache windows and ambiguous issuer routing in multi-tenant systems where several identity providers are accepted.

## Detection and Verification
- Manual checks: confirm strict issuer-to-JWKS binding and disallow untrusted key URLs
- Dynamic checks: attempt key confusion with alternate issuer and kid collisions
- Telemetry indicators: sudden key-set changes and token acceptance spikes by uncommon issuers
- False positive control: verify protected endpoint access using token signed by attacker-controlled key

## Secure Implementation
Pin JWKS endpoints per issuer, isolate cache entries by issuer and tenant context, and reject dynamic key URL hints from tokens. Enforce strict algorithm policy and require deterministic key rotation workflows with signed change controls.

## Hardening Checklist
- [x] Issuer-bound JWKS allowlist and transport pinning
- [x] Cache isolation by issuer and tenant context
- [x] Rejection of token-supplied key URL metadata
- [x] kid collision and algorithm confusion regression tests
- [x] Alerting for abnormal JWKS delta and cache miss bursts

## Mapping and References
- OWASP: JWT and API security recommendations
- CWE: CWE-347, CWE-295
- WSTG: authentication and token validation workflows
- ASVS: cryptographic key management and auth controls

## References
1. https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc7517
2. https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc7519
3. https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/JSON_Web_Token_for_Java_Cheat_Sheet.html
4. https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/347.html
author: Security Team

title: Metadata Service SSRF Bypass: IMDSv2 Enforcement and Egress Isolation
excerpt: How to close common cloud metadata exfiltration paths that survive basic SSRF filtering.
content:
# Metadata Service SSRF Bypass: IMDSv2 Enforcement and Egress Isolation

## Audience and Level
- Target audience: cloud platform teams, backend security engineers, SREs
- Difficulty: specialist
- Estimated read time: 15 minutes

## Why This Matters
SSRF remains one of the fastest routes from web-layer bugs to cloud credential theft. Even when teams block obvious metadata IP requests, attackers can still pivot through proxy chains, DNS rebinding, and redirect handling weaknesses to reach instance identity services.

## Threat Model Snapshot
- Assets at risk: temporary cloud credentials, service identity tokens, control-plane access
- Entry points: URL fetch features, webhook validators, image importers, PDF renderers
- Trust boundaries: application request parsing to outbound network execution
- Typical attacker goals: extract credentials and escalate to cloud account abuse

## Attack Mechanics
Attackers abuse permissive URL parsers, follow-redirect behavior, and resolver races to bypass naive deny lists. In cloud workloads they target metadata endpoints through alternate encodings or internal proxy hops and then replay harvested tokens against management APIs.

## Detection and Verification
- Manual checks: inspect all outbound fetch code paths and URL normalization logic
- Dynamic checks: DNS rebinding, redirect chains, and alternate host encoding payloads
- Telemetry indicators: unusual calls to link-local ranges and sudden STS token use
- False positive control: prove retrieval of metadata token or role credentials through SSRF path

## Secure Implementation
Require hardened metadata protocols such as IMDSv2 where available, disable unnecessary metadata access, and route egress through policy-enforcing gateways. Apply network-level blocks for link-local metadata destinations and implement explicit outbound destination allowlists in application logic.

## Hardening Checklist
- [x] Enforced IMDSv2 and disabled legacy metadata modes
- [x] Outbound network allowlists for URL fetch features
- [x] Redirect and DNS rebinding resistant URL validation
- [x] Link-local egress denied at network policy layer
- [x] Cloud credential use analytics with anomaly alerts

## Mapping and References
- OWASP: SSRF and cloud security hardening guidance
- CWE: CWE-918, CWE-441
- WSTG: server-side request testing methodology
- ASVS: secure architecture and network trust controls

## References
1. https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Server_Side_Request_Forgery_Prevention_Cheat_Sheet.html
2. https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/v42/
3. https://portswigger.net/web-security/ssrf
4. https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/918.html
author: Security Team

title: HTTP Request Desynchronization in H2-H1 Gateways: CL.0 and Downgrade Edge Cases
excerpt: Specialist methodology for validating request boundary consistency across modern proxy stacks.
content:
# HTTP Request Desynchronization in H2-H1 Gateways: CL.0 and Downgrade Edge Cases

## Audience and Level
- Target audience: edge platform engineers, backend security reviewers, offensive testers
- Difficulty: specialist
- Estimated read time: 16 minutes

## Why This Matters
Classic request smuggling guidance is no longer enough when architectures mix HTTP/2 clients, HTTP/1 backends, and layered reverse proxies. Small parser mismatches can still desynchronize request boundaries and allow cache poisoning, credential theft, or internal request forgery.

## Threat Model Snapshot
- Assets at risk: authenticated sessions, backend routing integrity, shared cache correctness
- Entry points: front proxies, CDN edges, API gateways, service mesh ingress
- Trust boundaries: protocol translation layers and chained request parsers
- Typical attacker goals: inject shadow requests and hijack downstream responses

## Attack Mechanics
Attackers test parser disagreement around content-length interpretation, pseudo-header translation, and stream-to-request mapping during protocol downgrade. They combine timing probes with crafted header sets to confirm desync and then weaponize for targeted response queue poisoning.

## Detection and Verification
- Manual checks: document parser behavior and canonicalization per hop
- Dynamic checks: CL.0, TE interactions, and H2 downgrade desync probes
- Telemetry indicators: anomalous 400 or 502 clusters and queue corruption signatures
- False positive control: demonstrate controlled desync leading to cross-user response impact

## Secure Implementation
Normalize and reserialize requests at trust boundaries, reject ambiguous framing, and enforce a single authoritative parsing model across tiers. Keep proxy firmware current and run synthetic desync test suites before configuration rollouts.

## Hardening Checklist
- [x] Strict rejection of ambiguous request framing
- [x] Protocol translation normalization at each boundary
- [x] Continuous desync regression tests in staging
- [x] Proxy and gateway patch cadence tied to security advisories
- [x] Incident playbook for request queue contamination events

## Mapping and References
- OWASP: injection and misconfiguration risk controls
- CWE: CWE-444, CWE-436
- WSTG: transport and server-side testing techniques
- ASVS: secure communications and parser consistency controls

## References
1. https://portswigger.net/research/http-desync-attacks-request-smuggling-reborn
2. https://portswigger.net/web-security/request-smuggling
3. https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/v42/
4. https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/444.html
author: Security Team

title: Micro-Frontend Authorization Drift: BFF Boundaries, Scope Partitioning, and Forced Browsing
excerpt: How distributed UI ownership can silently reintroduce broken access control across product domains.
content:
# Micro-Frontend Authorization Drift: BFF Boundaries, Scope Partitioning, and Forced Browsing

## Audience and Level
- Target audience: frontend platform owners, API teams, product security engineers
- Difficulty: specialist
- Estimated read time: 14 minutes

## Why This Matters
Micro-frontend architectures improve team velocity, but decentralized ownership can fragment authorization assumptions. Attackers exploit inconsistent route guards and backend-for-frontend shortcuts to access functions not exposed in primary navigation flows.

## Threat Model Snapshot
- Assets at risk: administrative workflows, cross-tenant records, sensitive business actions
- Entry points: hidden routes, stale feature flags, BFF aggregation endpoints
- Trust boundaries: client-side route controls to server-side authorization decisions
- Typical attacker goals: forced browsing and horizontal or vertical privilege escalation

## Attack Mechanics
Attackers enumerate route manifests, source maps, and API patterns to discover unlinked capabilities. They replay privileged calls against BFF endpoints that rely on UI gating rather than policy enforcement and pivot across domain-specific tokens with overly broad scopes.

## Detection and Verification
- Manual checks: map every sensitive operation to explicit backend authorization policy
- Dynamic checks: forced browsing and direct API invocation across role profiles
- Telemetry indicators: forbidden endpoint bursts followed by successful edge-case actions
- False positive control: verify unauthorized state change via hidden route or direct BFF call

## Secure Implementation
Treat UI authorization only as usability, never security. Enforce server-side policy at every mutation and sensitive read path, keep scopes least-privileged per domain, and require contract tests proving denied access for unsupported roles.

## Hardening Checklist
- [x] Server-side authorization for all BFF and domain APIs
- [x] Least-privilege token scopes per micro-frontend domain
- [x] Forced browsing tests for hidden routes and stale flags
- [x] Policy-as-code checks in merge pipelines
- [x] Role matrix drift reviews each release cycle

## Mapping and References
- OWASP: broken access control and architectural security controls
- CWE: CWE-285, CWE-639
- WSTG: authorization and access control test workflows
- ASVS: access control verification requirements

## References
1. https://owasp.org/Top10/2025/
2. https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/v42/
3. https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/285.html
4. https://owasp.org/www-project-application-security-verification-standard/
author: Security Team

title: Server-Side Prototype Pollution Sinks: Validation Gaps and Runtime Guard Rails
excerpt: Advanced detection and prevention patterns for server-side prototype pollution across API handlers and merge utilities.
content:
# Server-Side Prototype Pollution Sinks: Validation Gaps and Runtime Guard Rails

## Audience and Level
- Target audience: backend engineers, Node.js platform teams, secure code reviewers
- Difficulty: specialist
- Estimated read time: 15 minutes

## Why This Matters
Prototype pollution is frequently treated as a frontend issue, yet server-side sinks can alter authorization checks, request routing, and security middleware behavior. Exploitation becomes severe when polluted properties influence feature flags, trust decisions, or command construction.

## Threat Model Snapshot
- Assets at risk: authorization integrity, execution safety, service availability
- Entry points: deep merge utilities, JSON patch handlers, schema-less parsers
- Trust boundaries: user-controlled object graphs to shared runtime structures
- Typical attacker goals: tamper security logic and trigger privileged code paths

## Attack Mechanics
Attackers inject special keys like __proto__, constructor, or prototype through nested payloads and rely on unsafe merge or clone behavior. They then probe downstream effects where inherited properties modify policy outcomes or input validation expectations.

## Detection and Verification
- Manual checks: inventory merge libraries and custom object composition utilities
- Dynamic checks: nested pollution payloads against critical API workflows
- Telemetry indicators: anomalous object shape errors and unexpected policy toggles
- False positive control: prove security-relevant behavior change caused by polluted property

## Secure Implementation
Use schema validation that blocks dangerous keys at every depth, adopt safe merge utilities, and freeze sensitive configuration objects where feasible. Add runtime assertions for security-critical defaults and pin dependency versions against known prototype pollution advisories.

## Hardening Checklist
- [x] Deep schema validation with blocked meta-properties
- [x] Safe merge and clone primitives in shared utilities
- [x] Security-critical object freezing and immutability checks
- [x] Dependency policy for prototype pollution CVE response
- [x] Regression tests for pollution payload variants

## Mapping and References
- OWASP: injection and secure coding guidance
- CWE: CWE-1321, CWE-915
- WSTG: input validation and server-side test methods
- ASVS: secure coding and dependency governance controls

## References
1. https://portswigger.net/web-security/prototype-pollution
2. https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/v42/
3. https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/1321.html
4. https://owasp.org/Top10/2025/
author: Security Team
